beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam, in for Bear. Give Claude a PowerPoint and ask it to make it look like Anthropic made it. What comes back is a slide deck in the exact Anthropic palette — that dark near-black, the cream, the terracotta orange. Poppins on the headings, Lora on the body. Not a guess. The actual brand. A twelve-hundred word instruction file told Claude exactly what to do. Let me show you the file.

props.command: Apply Anthropic brand styling to this deck. Use the brand-guidelines skill.
output: Applying via python-pptx — all shapes, all slides, all text.

beats.act: Act 1 — what a skill is / the anatomy
beats.narration_text: A skill is a folder Claude reads before it works. This one is called brand-guidelines, and it contains one file: SKILL.md. No scripts, no templates, no asset bundles. Just the brand specification in plain language. The colors, the fonts, the rules for applying them — all written as instructions Claude follows. The brand guide became the instruction set. That is the whole design.

The pipeline is three nodes. A document comes in — a PowerPoint, a Word file, a PDF. Claude reads the brand-guidelines skill before it touches it. On the way out: every heading is Poppins, every body line is Lora, every shape picks from a three-color accent rotation, and the backgrounds snap to either dark or light brand tone. One instruction file, applied universally. The pipeline has no loops, no conditionals. It reads, then it writes.

This is the Anthropic brand palette, rendered live from the hex values in the SKILL.md. Dark: one-four-one-four-one-three — primary text and dark backgrounds. Light: faf9f5 — the cream. Mid gray: b0aea5. Light gray: e8e6dc. Then the accents: orange d97757 — the primary. Blue 6a9bcc — secondary. Green 788c5d — tertiary. Non-text shapes cycle through those three in order. The skill doesn't approximate — it gives Claude the exact RGB triples. This is the brand, not a mood board.

Typography. Poppins on headings — that's anything twenty-four points and above. Lora on everything else. The skill includes fallback chains: Arial when Poppins isn't installed, Georgia when Lora isn't. Both are standard system fonts, so this works everywhere. The size threshold is the interesting choice: twenty-four points isn't 'large' — it's the crossover where text reads as a heading rather than a caption. The skill made a decision and baked it in.

beats.act: Act 4 — the design tell / Teardown moment
beats.narration_text: Here is the Teardown moment. This skill is entirely data, no logic. The instruction file CONTAINS the brand — the exact hex values, the exact font names, the exact threshold — and Claude's job is to apply it mechanically. There is no 'be creative' anywhere in this file. That is intentional. Brand consistency is a constraint problem, not a design problem. The skill treats it like one. What it gets right: repeatability — run it twice, same output. What it bites: it cannot adapt to context. Anthropic's cream doesn't read well on projectors. The skill doesn't know that.

Brand-guidelines is a brand specification file made executable. You give Claude a document; Claude gives back a document that looks like Anthropic made it. The skill makes no judgment calls — it applies the spec, exactly, every time. That is its value. That is also its limit.

artifactLines: Brand specification made executable — the SKILL.md IS the brand guide
No judgment calls — same input → same output, every run
Limit: cannot adapt to context (projector, print, dark mode)

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: 'I have a deck I want to make look like it came from Anthropic. Apply the brand-guidelines skill. Start with the color system — tell me what you're changing and why before you write any code.' That last clause matters. Asking Claude to explain before acting surfaces the skill's actual constraint logic — when it explains what it cannot adapt, you know where the real work is.

props.command: I have a deck I want to make look like it came from Anthropic. Apply the brand-guidelines skill. Start with the color system — tell me what you're changing and why before you write any code.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Branded. Liam, in for Bear.
